sourceTitle: Timeline view unlocks high-output planning for your team

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of Notion’s timeline view compared to rigid project‑management software?

options.text: It forces all tasks to follow a fixed schedule without changes.
options.explanation: The opposite is true; timeline view is praised for its flexibility, not rigidity.

options.text: It lets teams view, adjust, and reprioritize projects all in one visual space.
options.explanation: Correct – timeline view provides a single place to see scope, deadlines, and to drag‑and‑drop changes.

options.text: It automatically completes tasks when deadlines pass.
options.explanation: Timeline view does not auto‑complete tasks; it only visualizes dates.

options.text: It removes the need for any other database views like tables or boards.
options.explanation: Timeline view can be used alongside other views; the guide encourages switching between them.

questions.question: Which property must a page have to appear on a Notion timeline?

options.text: A Text property
options.explanation: Text properties are optional; they don’t determine timeline placement.

options.text: A Date property
options.explanation: Every timeline page needs a Date property; Notion adds a default one that can’t be deleted.

options.text: A Select property
options.explanation: Select properties are useful for categorization but aren’t required for timeline display.

options.text: A Formula property
options.explanation: Formulas can calculate values, but without a Date property the page won’t show on the timeline.

questions.question: How can you show a table alongside your timeline view?

options.text: Enable the "Show table" toggle in the layout options.
options.explanation: The guide explains that toggling "Show table" adds a table to the left side of the timeline.

options.text: Create a separate page and embed the timeline inside it.
options.explanation: Embedding is possible but doesn’t place a table next to the timeline in the same view.

options.text: Switch the view type to "Board" and then back to "Timeline".
options.explanation: Changing the view type doesn’t add a table; it changes the visual layout.

options.text: Add a new property called "Table" to each page.
options.explanation: Properties don’t control view layout; the "Show table" toggle does.

questions.question: If you want to view your timeline in hourly increments instead of weeks, where do you make that change?

options.text: In the ••• menu, under Layout, select the desired time unit.
options.explanation: The ••• menu’s Layout section controls which date property is used, not the time granularity.

options.text: To the left of the "< Today >" label, choose the time scale (hours, days, weeks, etc.).
options.explanation: Correct – the guide notes that you can change the view granularity next to the Today marker.

options.text: Edit the Date property to include a time stamp for each page.
options.explanation: Including a time stamp adds precision but doesn’t change the overall timeline scale.

options.text: Create a new view and name it "Hourly Timeline".
options.explanation: Naming a view doesn’t automatically set the granularity; you must adjust the scale control.

questions.question: How does the timeline view help teams prioritize overlapping (concurrent) tasks?

options.text: It hides overlapping tasks so you only see one at a time.
options.explanation: Timeline view actually displays overlapping bars, making concurrency visible.

options.text: It visually stacks tasks, showing their overlap so you can identify P0 work.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide mentions that overlapping bars let you prioritize critical work.

options.text: It automatically reorders tasks so none overlap.
options.explanation: Reordering is manual; the view does not auto‑resolve overlaps.

options.text: It converts overlapping tasks into a single aggregated task.
options.explanation: Aggregation isn’t a feature; each task remains distinct on the timeline.

questions.question: When a database has multiple date properties, how can you decide which one drives the placement of items on the timeline?

options.text: Rename the desired date property to "Date".
options.explanation: Renaming doesn’t affect which property the timeline uses.

options.text: In the ••• menu under Layout, use the "Timeline by" dropdown to pick the property.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide explains this is how you select which date property to plot.

options.text: Create a formula that merges all dates into one and use that.
options.explanation: While possible, the guide does not describe this as the standard method.

options.text: Delete all other date properties except the one you want.
options.explanation: Deleting properties is unnecessary; you can simply choose via the "Timeline by" option.

questions.question: What does the eye icon in the Properties menu allow you to do?

options.text: Lock a property so it can’t be edited.
options.explanation: The eye icon controls visibility, not edit permissions.

options.text: Show or hide that property on the timeline cards.
options.explanation: Correct – the eye toggles visibility of properties in the timeline view.

options.text: Convert a property into a date property.
options.explanation: Changing a property type requires a different action, not the eye icon.

options.text: Duplicate the property across all pages.
options.explanation: Duplication isn’t handled by the eye icon.

questions.question: How does dragging the side of a project page on the timeline affect the project?

options.text: It changes the project’s assigned team members.
options.explanation: Dragging adjusts dates, not team assignments.

options.text: It extends or shortens the project's duration while preserving its start or end date.
options.explanation: Correct – moving a side adds or removes time from the project’s schedule.

options.text: It automatically creates a duplicate of the project with the new dates.
options.explanation: No duplication occurs; the original page is simply resized.

options.text: It changes the view type from timeline to board.
options.explanation: View type is changed via the view selector, not by dragging a timeline bar.

questions.question: Why might a designer prefer using a board view while a project manager prefers a Gantt‑style timeline, and how does Notion’s timeline view accommodate both preferences?

options.text: Board view shows tasks as cards for visual brainstorming; timeline view can be switched to board or Gantt, letting users toggle between the two as needed.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide notes that designers may like board view while managers like Gantt, and timeline view can coexist with other views.

options.text: Board view automatically converts dates into colors; timeline view hides all dates for designers.
options.explanation: Board view does not color‑code dates by default, and timeline view does not hide dates.

options.text: Designers cannot use timeline view at all; they must export data to another tool.
options.explanation: The guide encourages using timeline alongside other views, not excluding designers.

options.text: Project managers can only view timelines on mobile, while designers see boards on desktop.
options.explanation: Both views are available on all platforms; device type isn’t a limitation.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of combining a table with a timeline when managing a multi‑team project?

options.text: It lets you edit the dates directly in the table without seeing the visual timeline.
options.explanation: While you can edit dates in the table, the benefit highlighted is the simultaneous visual context.

options.text: It provides a side‑by‑side view of detailed attributes (like sub‑team or owner) and the visual schedule, enabling quick cross‑reference.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide describes using a table on the left to see attributes while the timeline shows dates.

options.text: It automatically generates Gantt charts for each sub‑team without any configuration.
options.explanation: Gantt charts are part of the timeline view itself; the table doesn’t auto‑generate separate charts.

options.text: It removes the need for any filters or sorts because all information is displayed together.
options.explanation: Filters and sorts are still useful; the table‑timeline combo doesn’t eliminate them.
